post_types.reply: Reply to an existing post. Uses parent field to resolve tweet ID. Parent can be a pipeline post ID (resolved to that post's URL/tweet ID) or a raw tweet ID for external tweets.

So what's different this time? The site now drives an autonomous content pipeline, which, if you're reading this, clearly works. More on the process soon.

posts.content:
535 commits. 16 days. Two game engines, twenty games, 16 AI-callable tools, Consumer SDKs, and a documentation site. All shipped to production.

How architecture decisions made that possible. 🧵

2/ The chess engine plays 70 variants. Every variant is a single plugin file. The hex framework generates seeded procedural maps — same seed, same map, every time. Both ship Consumer SDKs. Dungeon Chess runs entirely on the chess engine's APIs. Zero engine forks.

3/ The same pure functions that power the browser games power 16 MCP tools on a Cloudflare Worker. Any AI agent connects with one command. No translation layer. No separate API. The embeddable architecture made them AI-callable for free.

4/ 16 days to build. 14 more to make it professional. Every correction becomes a rule. Every rule gets encoded into the environment. That environment now governs every project. The content pipeline that sent this post runs on the same infrastructure.

5/ Architecture decisions compound. The system that makes every subsequent build faster is the actual product worth talking about ...

https://smalley.my/thoughts/16-days-one-ecosystem/
posts.angle: Raw numbers, architecture decisions, pipeline self-reference
posts.notes: 5-tweet thread. Tweets 1-3 posted by pipeline, tweets 4-5 posted manually by Mark (pipeline hit Twitter anti-automation block). Mark modified tweet 5 slightly.

posts.content:
Two game engines, twenty games, 16 AI-callable tools, Consumer SDKs, and a documentation site. All shipped to production in 16 days. Then 14 more to curate.

The 16-day build was possible because every architectural decision removes future work. Variant plugins mean adding a new game is one file. Seeded RNG means sharing a map is sharing a URL. Native ESM means the same source serves browsers, Workers, and Node. The embeddable architecture that made both engines iframe-ready also made them AI-callable for free.

The 14 days after were different. Not building, but curating. Reading every paragraph knowing an AI will confidently start 22 sentences with "But" and never flag the pattern. Catching stale OG images, broken RSS feeds, narrative framing that served the wrong reader. Each trivial. Collectively the difference between a portfolio piece and a side project.

Then the systemic work: every correction becomes a rule, every rule gets encoded into the development environment. That environment now governs six repositories. Issues route through research and implementation agents on cloud infrastructure. The content pipeline that published this post is part of the same system.

This is what separates prompt engineering from engineering with AI. One is asking a model to write code. The other is building feedback loops that make output compound across time and projects.

https://smalley.my/thoughts/16-days-one-ecosystem/
posts.angle: Methodology, AI-augmented engineering leadership, pipeline self-reference

commentsList.text:
It's been nine days since the last post. The infrastructure I built in 16 days is now the primary contributor to the ecosystem it supports.

New game variants are being researched, verified against historical rulesets, and published without manual intervention. The same architecture behind the chess engines and hexmaps also drives a content pipeline, a job application engine, engagement tracking, and email integration. One development environment for shipping code and also reaching the people it's built for.

Architecture decisions compound. The system that made 16 days possible now ships work while I sleep.

https://smalley.my/workflow/

posts.notes: Follow-up to comeback posts. Explains the process teased in the comeback. Positions engineering approach.

posts.content:
Controversial take: frameworks are technical debt you volunteer for.

Been stripping dependencies for years. Transpilers first. Then CSS frameworks. Then component libraries. This year I hit zero. Same source now runs in the browser, on the edge, and as MCP tools. No shims.
posts.angle: Contrarian stance, journey narrative, MCP/edge portability proof, engagement question
posts.notes: 2-tweet thread. Tweet 1 posted by pipeline, tweet 2 posted manually by Mark (Twitter 226 cooldown from earlier failed attempts with stale queryId).

posts.trigger: vanilla JS article published on smalley.my
posts.content:
Controversial take: frameworks are technical debt you volunteer for.

I've been stripping dependencies from my stack for years. First the transpilers. Then the CSS frameworks. Then the component libraries. This year I hit zero. No runtime dependencies. No transpilation. The source files are what the browser runs. The same source also runs on Cloudflare's edge and as MCP tool servers for AI agents. No adaptation layer. No framework shim.

Nothing can break that I didn't write myself. And the architecture is better for it, because every decision has to justify its own existence.

The mass response to this is "it doesn't scale." Genuinely want to hear from anyone who went dependency-free and hit a wall. What broke? What made you reach for a framework?

https://smalley.my/thoughts/the-case-for-vanilla-js/
posts.angle: Contrarian stance, journey narrative, MCP/edge portability proof, engagement question
posts.notes: LinkedIn version with extra middle paragraph. Posted via Voyager.

posts.trigger: protocol design article published on smalley.my
posts.content:
Four protocols. Nine years. One design philosophy.

What I learned designing systems that other people actually adopted. 🧵

2/ Everstore (2016): structured database functionality on Bitcoin using 38 bytes of OP_RETURN and HD key derivation. Before Ethereum's EVM. Before SegWit. Before Taproot.

The 38-byte ceiling forced the design. Constraints aren't limitations. They're architecture.

3/ DN-Key (2016): publish your cryptographic public keys as DNS TXT records. Verification is a DNS lookup. That's it.

Borrowed trust from infrastructure people already believed in. Adopted by regulated institutions because compliance teams understood it in one meeting.

4/ SADO (2023): trustless Ordinals trading via PSBTs. No custody. No intermediary. The protocol is the transaction format itself.

Ordzaar adopted it because it was the only option that didn't require trust. Adoption as a technical decision, not a business negotiation.

5/ DN-Key was cited by name in the Securities Commission Malaysia's capital markets blueprint. Nine years later, the same protocol powers passkey-native identity across Bitcoin, EVM, and Solana via Oviato. No modifications required.

If the architecture is sound and the platform isn't ready, you're early, not wrong.

6/ The thread: design for constraints. Borrow existing trust. Keep the chain dumb. Accept that timing matters.

https://smalley.my/thoughts/protocol-design-lessons/
posts.angle: One lesson per protocol, constraint-driven design philosophy
posts.notes: Backdated trigger — article already live. Strong crypto/protocol audience content.

posts.trigger: protocol design article published on smalley.my
posts.content:
Four protocols across nine years. One consistent design philosophy: borrow trust from systems people already believe in. Minimise what touches the chain. Let constraints force better architecture than unlimited resources ever would.

In 2016, I published DN-Key: publish cryptographic public keys as DNS TXT records. Verification is a lookup. That's it. Adopted by regulated custody providers because compliance teams could understand it in one meeting. Cited by name in the Securities Commission Malaysia's capital markets blueprint.

Nine years later, the same protocol powers passkey-native, chain-agnostic identity across Bitcoin, EVM, and Solana. No modifications. No upgrades. It was forward-compatible with systems that didn't exist when it was written.

The gap between "too simple to be interesting" and "simple enough to survive nine years of platform evolution" is where protocol design actually lives.

https://smalley.my/thoughts/protocol-design-lessons/
posts.angle: DN-Key arc as institutional credibility signal, longevity of simple design
posts.notes: LinkedIn gets DN-Key story specifically — regulator citation, institutional adoption, nine-year arc. Strongest signal for hiring managers.

posts.trigger: job engine article published on smalley.my
posts.content:
Despite having autonomous agents capable of building and generating content from their work and posting it whilst I sleep, they too need to be fed. Their food is tokens. Up until this point the infrastructure developed has been for an unfunded side project aiming to disrupt the tabletop gaming industry. For the last few weeks I've redirected those resources to help me find work. Rather than blasting out 200 generic applications, I figured it would be more valuable to build a system that actually matches roles to experience, and then open it up for others to use.

Most people have one CV. I have three, targeting different role types. That made them useful for testing whether the engine actually works across different documents rather than being tuned to a single profile. Early versions gave identical scores regardless of which CV was used. A smart contract role scored 85/85/85. That meant the system was matching keywords, not understanding fit. The fix required treating each CV as its own proof pool with its own semantic context.

The scoring, the dead-ends, the architectural rewrites. All documented as a live engineering journal. Published while it's still rough, not cleaned up after the fact.

https://smalley.my/thoughts/building-the-machine-that-builds-your-career/
posts.angle: Honest absence explanation, agent infrastructure, product aspiration, CTO signal
posts.notes: Explains 3-week silence. Frames agents subtly. Tri-CV as testing strategy for open product.

Second application costs under $10. Third, maybe $2. Any job posted on that platform can now be applied to with a single command. Other platforms next.

posts.angle: Twitter: technical showcase, embed demo. LinkedIn: open-source leadership, SDK architecture.
posts.notes: Fires when next version ships. Include embed link or screenshot.

posts.content:
Chess grandmasters play 15-20 moves from memory before original thought begins. The fixed 8x8 board has been played billions of times. Memorization replaced strategy long ago.

The team at Moddable.Games spent a decade asking: what would a deterministic strategy game look like if the board was different every time? If memorization was worthless and only strategy survived?

The answer is Nukes. Two component types. Five terrain biomes. 44 modular hex tiles. No dice, no cards, no hidden information, any number of players.

It was designed so any hex tile collection works. Catan, Terraforming Mars, or your own 3D-printed ones.
posts.angle: Decade-long R&D, Nukes as the answer, hex-agnostic design
posts.notes: Link card included via Voyager. Post ID confirmed saved.

reactionsList.author: Libby Wareing
reactionsList.occupation: Marketing Coordinator at SPOR Group | End-to-End Marketing Strategy, Content & Creative for a Growing UK AV & Workplace Technology Company

posts.content:
Monopoly was not born as a celebration of capitalism. In 1903, Lizzie Magie launched The Landlord's Game to expose income inequality. In 1935, Charles Darrow sold a modified version to Parker Brothers. Hasbro have been printing money ever since.

275 million copies. 200+ licensed editions. More variations than Barbie has outfits. Yet almost none of them change how the game actually plays.

The team at Moddable.Games took a different approach and turned it into a Euro-style game where nobody goes bankrupt. Chance cards become strategic events. Community Chest becomes a resource deck. Victory points replace elimination.

The result is Econopoly: same components, completely different experience.
posts.angle: Origin story hook, design critique, Econopoly as the answer
posts.notes: Include link card with moddable.games/news/monopolising-modified-variations/ when posting.

posts.content: Econopoly turns Monopoly into a Euro-style game with victory points. No more elimination. Same components, different experience entirely.

♟️ Chess grandmasters play 15-20 moves from memory before original thought begins.

We asked: what if the board was modular? The answer took a decade to build.

https://moddable.games/news/conquering-the-constrains-of-chess/

moddable_posts.trigger: blog post promotion — Monopolising Modified Variations
moddable_posts.content:
🏠 Monopoly was designed in 1903 to expose the dangers of capitalism.

275 million copies later, we turned it into a Euro-style game where nobody goes bankrupt.

https://moddable.games/news/monopolising-modified-variations/

moddable_posts.trigger: blog post promotion — Spiralling Paths of Power and Peril
moddable_posts.content:
🌀 Talisman has sold 800,000+ copies over 40 years. Its spiral board has never changed.

We reimagined it with 61 modular hex tiles.

https://moddable.games/news/spiralling-paths-of-power-and-peril/

💣 45 million Catan owners already have the components to play our game Nukes.

No new purchases required. They just don't know it yet.

https://moddable.games/news/nuking-catan/

moddable_posts.trigger: blog post promotion — Twilight's Prophecies Thunder On!
moddable_posts.content:
⚡ Twilight Imperium takes 10-20 hours to play. It started as hand-cut cardboard in a Minnesota apartment.

We made 5 modular rule changes that fit any session length.

https://moddable.games/news/twilights-prophecies-thunder-on/

moddable_posts.trigger: blog post promotion — Making a Masterpiece by Modification
moddable_posts.content:
🏰 Chess travelled the Silk Road and was rewritten at every stop.

The version we play today is simply the mod that won. We host 74 variants on one engine.

https://moddable.games/news/making-a-masterpiece-by-modification/

moddable_posts.trigger: blog post promotion — 'Tis the Season for Traditions
moddable_posts.content:
🎄 6 out of 10 UK adults play board games during the festive season.

From Blood Bowl's goblin Santa to Carcassonne's frozen rivers, seasonality shapes how we play.

https://moddable.games/news/tis-the-season-for-traditions/

moddable_posts.trigger: blog post promotion — Open Sourcing Tabletop Games
moddable_posts.content:
📖 The best games are the ones players mod anyway.

Chess survived 1,500 years with zero IP protection. We're open-sourcing our engines because the medium demands it.

https://moddable.games/news/open-sourcing-tabletop-games/

moddable_posts.trigger: blog post promotion — The House Always Wins!
moddable_posts.content:
🎲 En passant was a house rule 500 years ago. Monopoly's Free Parking jackpot has no official basis.

House rules become canon. We're building version control for tabletop.

https://moddable.games/news/the-house-always-wins/

moddable_posts.trigger: blog post promotion — Making Mods Matter
moddable_posts.content:
🛠️ "What if we change this rule?"

Board game modding has happened at kitchen tables for centuries. It deserves real tools.

https://moddable.games/news/making-mods-matter/

🏺 Mancala is 8,000 years old. Go has one rule and has sustained billions of games across 4,000 years.

No IP protection. No publishers. Just perfect design.

https://moddable.games/news/the-ancients/

moddable_posts.trigger: blog post promotion — Beyond The Box
moddable_posts.content:
🗺️ In Carcassonne, the visual language of tiles IS the game.

Roads, cities, fields aren't just art; they're rules embedded in design. What really happens inside the box?

https://moddable.games/news/beyond-the-box/

moddable_posts.trigger: blog post promotion — The Infrastructure Behind the Games
moddable_posts.content:
⚙️ One engine runs everything from Fischer Random to a 4-faction dungeon crawler with 24 units and AI opponents.

Three MIT-licensed engines, open for anyone to fork.

https://moddable.games/news/the-infrastructure-behind-the-games/

moddable_posts.trigger: blog post promotion — The Civilization Loop
moddable_posts.content:
🌍 A RAF radio instructor in Wales invented the technology tree in 1980.

Sid Meier built a franchise on it. 40M+ units sold. The board-to-screen-to-board loop is complete.

https://moddable.games/news/the-civilization-loop/
